en.camerata-περιγραφή-1: Camerata is an extinct subclass of stalked crinoids that flourished during the Paleozoic era. They first appeared in the Early Ordovician and persisted until the end-Permian mass extinction.
en.camerata-περιγραφή-2: What distinguishes Camerata from other crinoids (their sister group Pentacrinoidea, which includes all living species) is the construction of a rigid, heavily sutured calyx. Proximal brachial plates and often interradial plates are firmly incorporated into the cup, creating a solidly fused theca. The tegmen is also heavily plated and rigid, forming a protective roof over the mouth. Camerates typically possess a medial plate series in the posterior interray and support numerous free, often pinnulate arms arising above the rigid cup.
en.camerata-περιγραφή-3: These features produced a more heavily armored and less flexible crown compared to the more open calyces of non-camerate crinoids. As dominant suspension feeders in many Paleozoic shallow marine communities, camerates left an abundant fossil record, particularly in Ordovician to Carboniferous deposits. They did not survive the end-Permian extinction, unlike the pentacrinoid lineage that gave rise to modern crinoids.
